follow.definition: A hit in a ball game. The ball moves and then follows another ball.
follow.exampleSentence: He plays a follow and the ball moves well.
follow.contextPassage: A follow is a move in a ball game. You hit one ball and it goes after the other ball.
distractors: A move where the ball stops very fast. It does not go far.
A move where the ball goes to the side. It turns left or right.
A move where you start the ball game. It is the first hit.
A move where the ball goes back. It moves the other way.

five number.definition: The number after four. It is 5.
five number.exampleSentence: I have five apples in my bag.
five number.contextPassage: You use five to count things. Five comes after four and before six.
distractors: The number after five. It is 6.
The number after three. It is 4.
The number after six. It is 7.

food.definition: Things you eat to live and grow.
food.exampleSentence: I eat good food every day.
food.contextPassage: Food gives your body energy. Rice, bread, and fruit are all food.
distractors: Water or juice you drink every day.
A place where you buy things to eat.
The time when you sit down to eat.

foot.definition: The part at the bottom of your leg. You walk on it.
foot.contextPassage: You have two feet. You use them to walk and run.
distractors: The part of your body you use to hold things.
The top part of your leg, above your knee.
The part of your body you use to see.

football.definition: A sport where two teams kick a ball. They try to put it in a goal.
football.exampleSentence: I play football with my friends.
football.contextPassage: Football is a very popular sport. You play it with a big round ball.
distractors: A sport where you hit a ball with a bat.
A sport where you swim as fast as you can.
A sport where you throw a ball into a high net.

for.definition: You use this word to say who gets something or why you do something.
for.contextPassage: You can say 'this is for me' or 'I do this for school'. It shows a reason or a person.
distractors: You use this word to say where something is.
You use this word to say when something happens.
You use this word to say how big something is.

forget.definition: To not remember something you knew before.
forget.exampleSentence: I forget my friend's phone number.
forget.contextPassage: Sometimes you forget words in a new language. You can write things down so you do not forget.
distractors: To learn something new for the first time.
To think about something again and again.
To find something you lost a long time ago.

forty number.definition: The number 40. It comes after thirty-nine.
forty number.exampleSentence: There are forty students in my school.
forty number.contextPassage: Forty is four times ten. You write it as 40.
distractors: The number 14. It comes after thirteen.
The number 4. It comes after three.
The number 30. It comes after twenty-nine.

four number.definition: The number 4. It comes after three.
four number.exampleSentence: I have four cats at home.
four number.contextPassage: Four is two plus two. You write it as 4.
distractors: The number 5. It comes after four.
The number 3. It comes after two.
The number 40. It comes after thirty-nine.

fourteen number.definition: The number 14. It comes after thirteen.
fourteen number.exampleSentence: My sister is fourteen years old.
fourteen number.contextPassage: Fourteen is ten plus four. You write it as 14.
distractors: The number 40. It comes after thirty-nine.
The number 4. It comes after three.
The number 41. It comes after forty.

fourth number.definition: Number four in a line. It comes after the third.
fourth number.exampleSentence: I am fourth in the line today.
fourth number.contextPassage: You use fourth to say where someone is in order. April is the fourth month of the year.
distractors: Number three in a line. It comes after the second.
Number five in a line. It comes after the fourth.
Number two in a line. It comes after the first.

free.definition: Something you get without paying money for it.
free.exampleSentence: This book is free for all students.
free.contextPassage: Free means you do not pay. You can get free food at some schools.
distractors: Something that costs a lot of money to buy.
Something that is very small and easy to carry.
Something old that you do not want anymore.

friday.definition: A day of the week. It comes after Thursday.
friday.exampleSentence: I go to the park on Friday.
friday.contextPassage: Friday is the fifth day of the school week. Many people are happy on Friday.
distractors: A day of the week that comes after Saturday.
The first day of the week. It comes before Tuesday.
A day of the week that comes after Sunday.

friend.definition: A person you like very much. You love to be with them.
friend.exampleSentence: My best friend lives next to me.
friend.contextPassage: A friend is not your family. You play and talk with your friend.
distractors: A person in your family, like a sister or brother.
A person who teaches you at school every day.
A person you do not know and never talk to.

friendly.definition: A game where the score does not count. Teams play for fun, not to win points.
friendly.exampleSentence: We play a friendly game today.
friendly.contextPassage: A friendly is not a real match. No team wins or loses points in it.
distractors: A big game where the winner gets a prize.
The last game of a long year of playing.
A game where two players fight for first place.

from.definition: It shows where someone or something comes. It tells you the start place.
from.exampleSentence: She is from a small town.
from.contextPassage: You use 'from' to say where something started. It can be a place or a person.
distractors: It shows where someone is going next.
It tells you how long something takes.
It shows who is next to you.

front.definition: The side of something you see first. It is the part that faces you.
front.exampleSentence: She stands at the front of the class.
front.contextPassage: The front is the forward part of something. It is the opposite of the back.
distractors: The part of something you cannot see at all.
The side of something on the left.
The very top of a tall thing.

fruit.definition: A sweet food that grows on a plant or tree. You can eat it.
fruit.exampleSentence: I eat a piece of fruit every day.
fruit.contextPassage: An apple is a fruit. A banana is a fruit too.
distractors: A food that grows under the ground.
A drink you make from plants and water.
A hard part of a tree you cannot eat.

full.definition: It has no more space inside. You cannot put more in it.
full.exampleSentence: My bag is full of books.
full.contextPassage: A full cup has water to the top. You cannot add more water to it.
distractors: It has a lot of space and nothing inside.
It is broken and you cannot use it.
It is very old and very small.

fun.definition: A good happy feeling when you enjoy something. It feels great.
fun.exampleSentence: We have a lot of fun at the park.
fun.contextPassage: You have fun when you play or laugh. Games and friends give you fun.
distractors: A bad feeling when something is hard to do.
A feeling you get when you are very tired.
A sad feeling when you are alone.

funny.definition: Something you say to make people laugh. It is a small joke.
funny.exampleSentence: He tells a funny at lunch.
funny.contextPassage: A funny is a short joke or silly thing you say. People smile or laugh when they hear it.
distractors: A long sad story you tell to friends.
A question you ask to get an answer.
A kind word you say to help someone.

game.definition: An activity where you play and have fun. It can have rules and a winner.
game.exampleSentence: We play a game after school.
game.contextPassage: Football is a game. Cards and board games are games too.
distractors: A time when you sit and do nothing.
A hard task you do at work or school.
A long walk you do with your family.

garden.definition: An outdoor place with plants and flowers. People grow things there.
garden.exampleSentence: My mum has flowers in her garden.
garden.contextPassage: You can grow food in a garden. Some people grow vegetables or flowers there.
distractors: A big room inside a house with many plants.
A place outside where children play with toys.
A small shop where you buy flowers and plants.

get.definition: A young animal born to a parent animal. It is the baby of an animal.
get.exampleSentence: The dog has a strong and healthy get.
get.contextPassage: Animals have a get when they have babies. A get looks like its parent.
distractors: An old animal that cannot walk or run.
A plant that grows near water and trees.
A small bird that lives near houses.

girl.definition: A young female person. She is not a boy.
girl.exampleSentence: The girl has a red bag.
girl.contextPassage: A girl can be a baby, a child, or a young woman. She grows up to be a woman.
distractors: A young male person who goes to school.

key.exampleSentence: I use my key to open the front door.
key.contextPassage: A key is small and hard. You carry your key in your bag or pocket.
distractors: A small round object you push to make something work.
A long flat thing you use to eat food.
A thin tool you use to write or draw.

kilometre.definition: A way to say how far something is. One kilometre is one thousand metres.
kilometre.exampleSentence: The school is one kilometre from my house.
kilometre.contextPassage: We use kilometres to talk about long distances. Roads and maps often show kilometres.
distractors: A way to say how heavy something is.
A way to say how tall a person is in small steps.
A way to say how fast something moves.

kind.definition: A person who is good and nice to other people. They help and care.
kind.exampleSentence: She is very kind to her friends.
kind.contextPassage: A kind person makes you feel good. Kind people help others and share.
distractors: A person who is very loud and talks a lot.
A person who is very fast and strong.
A person who is very big and tall.

kitchen.definition: A room in a home where you cook food. It has a stove and a sink.
kitchen.exampleSentence: My mum cooks dinner in the kitchen.
kitchen.contextPassage: The kitchen is an important room in a home. You can make food and drinks in the kitchen.
distractors: A room in a home where you sleep at night.
A room in a home where you wash your body.
A room in a home where you sit and watch TV.

know.definition: The things you have in your mind. You have learned and you understand.
know.exampleSentence: She has a lot of know about animals.
know.contextPassage: You get know from books and school. When you study, your know gets bigger.
distractors: The feeling you have when you are very happy.
The things you can see with your eyes.
The words you say when you talk to someone.

land.definition: The dry part of the earth. It is not water or sea.
land.exampleSentence: The ship came close to the land.
land.contextPassage: Animals and people live on land. Land can be flat like a field or high like a mountain.
distractors: The part of the earth that is under the sea.
The air and sky above the earth.
The water that flows fast between two sides.

language.definition: The words people use to talk and write. People in the same group use it together.
language.exampleSentence: English is a language many people speak.
language.contextPassage: Every country has a language. You learn a language so you can talk with other people.
distractors: The way a person moves their body to dance.
The sounds animals make to eat and sleep.
The numbers people use to count and add.

large.definition: A very old music sound. It is not used today.
large.exampleSentence: The large is an old music note.
large.contextPassage: People do not use this note now. It is from very old music books.

last.definition: It comes after all the others. There is nothing after it.
last.exampleSentence: This is the last apple in the box.
last.contextPassage: We say last for the final one in a group. After the last one, there are no more.

late.definition: A work time at the end of the day or at night.
late.exampleSentence: She works the late at the hospital.
late.contextPassage: Some people start work in the evening. They do the late shift.

later.definition: It is near the end of a time. Not early, not in the middle.
later.exampleSentence: We eat dinner in the later part of the day.
later.contextPassage: Later hours are close to the end. The later days of a trip are near the last day.

laugh.definition: A happy sound you make with your mouth. You make it when something is funny.
laugh.exampleSentence: I hear a big laugh from my friend.
laugh.contextPassage: A laugh is a happy sound. People make this sound when they are happy or see something funny.

learn.definition: The act of getting new knowledge. You know something new after it.
learn.exampleSentence: Reading is a good way to do a learn.
learn.contextPassage: A learn happens when you study something new. After a good learn, you know more than before.

leave.definition: To go away and let something stay there.
leave.exampleSentence: He will leave his bag on the chair.
leave.contextPassage: When you leave something, it stays in that place. You go, but the thing does not go with you.

left.definition: The side that is not the right side.
left.exampleSentence: The school is on the left.
left.contextPassage: Left is a direction. You can turn left or look to the left.

leg.definition: The long part of the body. You use it to stand and walk.
leg.exampleSentence: My dog has a hurt leg.
leg.contextPassage: People and animals have legs. We use our legs to walk and run.

lesson.definition: A time when you learn one thing at school. A teacher helps you.
lesson.exampleSentence: We have a math lesson today.
lesson.contextPassage: A lesson is one part of a school day. After the lesson, you know something new.

let.definition: When someone pays to live in your house or room.
let.exampleSentence: She has a let for her small flat.
let.contextPassage: A let means someone pays you to live in your home. You get money and they get a place to stay.

letter.definition: A shape you write or read. Letters make words.
letter.exampleSentence: The letter A is easy to write.
letter.contextPassage: There are 26 letters in English. We put letters together to make words.

library.definition: A place where you can get books to read. You can take books home from here.
library.exampleSentence: I get books at the library.
library.contextPassage: A library has many books. You can read there too.
distractors: A place where you buy books and pay for them.
A place where children go to learn and study every day.
A place where you can buy food and drinks.

life.definition: The time when a person, animal, or plant is alive. You have life when you eat, breathe, and grow.
life.exampleSentence: The cat has a good life.
life.contextPassage: People and animals have life. A rock does not have life.
distractors: The time when a person is asleep and not moving.
A long trip you take to a new place.
The work you do every day to earn money.

light.definition: The bright thing you see from the sun or a lamp. It helps you see things around you.
light.exampleSentence: The light in this room is very bright.
light.contextPassage: The sun gives us light every day. A lamp gives light at night.
distractors: A loud sound you hear when something falls down.
The warm feeling you get from fire or the sun.
A soft thing you put on a bed to sleep on.

like.definition: The things a person loves or enjoys. These are things that make you happy.
like.exampleSentence: Music is one of her big likes.
like.contextPassage: You can talk about your likes with friends. Food and games can be likes.
distractors: The things a person does not want or enjoy.
The things a person must do every day.
The things a person is very good at doing.

line.definition: A long thin mark you draw or see. It goes from one point to another.
line.exampleSentence: Draw a line on the paper.
line.contextPassage: You can draw a line with a pen. A road is like a long line.
distractors: A big round shape like a ball or the sun.
A flat square shape you can draw on paper.
A small dot you make with a pen or pencil.

lion.definition: A big wild cat with a loud roar. It lives in Africa and has brown fur.
lion.exampleSentence: The lion is a very big animal.
lion.contextPassage: A lion is a big strong cat. It lives in hot places in Africa.
distractors: A big grey animal with a very long nose.
A fast wild animal that looks like a big dog.
A very tall animal with a very long neck.

list.definition: A set of things written one after another. You write it to help you remember things.
list.exampleSentence: I have a list of things to buy.
list.contextPassage: You can write a list of food to buy. A list helps you remember.
distractors: A short letter you write to a friend.
A small book with many pictures inside.
A big paper with numbers and scores on it.

listen.definition: A time when you pay attention to a sound or someone talking. You use your ears to do this.
listen.exampleSentence: Give this song a listen tonight.
listen.contextPassage: A listen to music can make you happy. You have a listen when someone speaks.
distractors: A time when you look very hard at something.
A time when you touch and feel something new.
A time when you smell something around you.

little.definition: A small amount of something. It is not a lot.
little.exampleSentence: I want a little water, please.
little.contextPassage: A little food is a small amount. You say little when you do not want a lot.
distractors: A very large amount of something you have.
The same amount every time you have something.
All of something with nothing left.

live.definition: To be alive. People, animals, and plants live.
live.contextPassage: You live every day when you breathe and eat. Animals and people live on Earth.
distractors: To sleep for a very long time without waking.
To stop moving and become very cold and still.
To go to a new place and not come back.

long.definition: A big word that has a long sound inside it. The sound in the word takes more time to say.
long.exampleSentence: The word bone has a long vowel sound.
long.contextPassage: Long sounds take more time to say. The letter A in cake is a long sound.
distractors: A word that has a sound that is very quick and short.
A word that has two sounds right next to each other.
A word that has no sound at all inside it.

look.definition: To turn your eyes to something and see it. You do this when you want to see something.
look.contextPassage: You look at something with your eyes. You can look up, down, or around you.
distractors: To touch something with your hands to feel it.
To hear a sound with your ears very carefully.
To smell something near you with your nose.

lose.definition: To not have something anymore. You had it, but now it is gone.
lose.exampleSentence: I lose my keys all the time.
lose.contextPassage: You can lose things like a bag or a phone. You can also lose a game.
distractors: To find something you did not have before.
To give something to another person.
To keep something safe in a place.

lot.definition: A very big number or amount of something.
lot.exampleSentence: I have a lot of books at home.
lot.contextPassage: You can say a lot of food or a lot of people. It means there is very much of something.
distractors: A very small number of things.
Just one thing, not more.
Half of something big.

love.definition: A very strong, warm feeling you have for someone.
love.exampleSentence: I love my family very much.
love.contextPassage: You can feel love for a person or a pet. It is a very good and big feeling.
distractors: A small happy feeling when you eat good food.
A feeling you have when you are very tired.
A feeling you have when you are angry at someone.

lunch.definition: A meal you eat in the middle of the day.
lunch.exampleSentence: I eat lunch at school every day.
lunch.contextPassage: Lunch is after breakfast and before dinner. Many people eat lunch at 12 o'clock.
distractors: A meal you eat early in the morning.
A small drink you have before you sleep.
A big meal you eat late at night.

no one.exampleSentence: No one is in the classroom today.
no one.contextPassage: You use no one when zero people do something. For example, no one answered the door.
distractors: Every single person in a place.
One or two people in a group.
A very big group of people.

nobody.definition: Not one person. Zero people are there or do something.
nobody.contextPassage: Nobody means not one person at all. It is the same as no one.
distractors: A very important and well-known person.
All the people in a big group.
One person you know very well.

north.definition: One of four big directions. On a map, it is at the top.
north.exampleSentence: The city is to the north of here.
north.contextPassage: North is the opposite of south. You can use a map to find north.
distractors: The direction that is at the bottom of a map.
The direction to the right side of a map.
The direction to the left side of a map.

nose.definition: The part of your face you use to smell and breathe.
nose.exampleSentence: My nose is cold in the winter.
nose.contextPassage: Your nose is in the middle of your face. You use it to smell food and flowers.
distractors: The part of your face you use to eat and talk.
The two parts of your face you use to see things.
The two parts on the side of your head you use to hear.

not.definition: A word you use to say something is the opposite or wrong.
not.contextPassage: You use not to say no about something. For example, I am not tired.
distractors: A word you use to say yes to something.
A word you use to ask a question.
A word you use to talk about a big number.

note.definition: A short piece of writing to help you remember something.
note.exampleSentence: I write a note in my book.
note.contextPassage: A note is short, not a long text. You can write a note to remember a name or a number.
distractors: A very long story you read in a book.
A picture you draw with colors.
A loud sound you make with your mouth.

nothing.exampleSentence: There is nothing in the box.
nothing.contextPassage: Nothing means not any thing at all. For example, I have nothing in my bag.
distractors: A very big and important thing.
Many different things in one place.
One small and nice thing.

november.definition: The eleventh month of the year.
november.contextPassage: November comes after October. It is the month before December.
distractors: The tenth month of the year, before November.
The twelfth and last month of the year.
The ninth month of the year.

now.definition: This time. Not before. Not after. Right at this moment.
now.exampleSentence: I am eating my food now.
now.contextPassage: You say 'now' when something is happening today or at this time. It is not yesterday and not tomorrow.
distractors: A time that already happened, not today.
A time that will come soon, but not yet.
Every day, again and again, not just today.

number.definition: A word or sign that tells you how many. Like one, two, or three.
number.exampleSentence: I see the number five on the door.
number.contextPassage: You use a number to count things. Numbers help you know how many you have.
distractors: A word that is the name of a person or place.
A sign that tells you to stop or go.
A mark that shows how big or small something is.

nurse.definition: A person who helps sick people and works with a doctor.
nurse.exampleSentence: The nurse helps me when I am sick.
nurse.contextPassage: A nurse works in a hospital or a doctor's room. A nurse is kind and helps you feel better.
distractors: A person who teaches children in a school.
A person who makes food for you in a restaurant.
A person who drives you to the hospital in a big car.

o'clock.definition: A word you say after a number to tell the time. Like two o'clock.
o'clock.contextPassage: You use o'clock with numbers one to twelve. It tells you the exact hour on a clock.
distractors: A word that means a few minutes before the next hour.
A word that means half of an hour has passed.
A word that means you do not know what time it is.

october.definition: The tenth month of the year. It comes after September.
october.contextPassage: October has thirty-one days. In many places, it is cold in October.
distractors: The ninth month of the year, before October.
The eleventh month of the year, after October.
The last month of the year, in winter.

of.definition: A small word that shows something belongs to or comes from something.
of.exampleSentence: This is a photo of my family.
of.contextPassage: You use 'of' to connect two words. It shows one thing is part of another thing.
distractors: A small word that means you are moving to a place.
A small word that means two things are the same.
A small word that means something is on top of something.

off.definition: Away from a place. Not on. Not working.
off.exampleSentence: Please turn the light off at night.
off.contextPassage: You say 'off' when something stops. The TV is off means it is not working now.
distractors: Working and making light or sound right now.
Moving very fast toward a new place.
Staying in one place and not moving at all.

office.definition: A room where people sit and do work. People write and use computers there.
office.exampleSentence: My dad goes to the office every morning.
office.contextPassage: An office is a place for work, not for sleeping or eating. Many people in an office sit at a desk.
distractors: A room where sick people go to sleep and get better.
A big room where children go to learn and read books.
A place where people buy food and other things they need.

often.definition: Many times. Not just one time. Again and again.
often.exampleSentence: I often eat eggs for breakfast.
often.contextPassage: You say 'often' when something happens many times. It is not always, but it happens a lot.
distractors: One time only, not again after that.
Every single time, with no exception at all.
Not very many times, only one or two times.

oh exclam..definition: A word you say when you feel surprised or when you just understood something.
oh exclam..exampleSentence: Oh, I did not see you there!
oh exclam..contextPassage: You say 'oh' when something surprises you. You also say 'oh' when you learn something new.
distractors: A word you say when you want someone to be quiet and stop talking.
A word you say to ask someone to come closer to you.
A word you say when you finish talking and want to say goodbye.

ok exclam..definition: A word that means yes or good. It means you are happy with something.
ok exclam..exampleSentence: OK, I can go to the park!
ok exclam..contextPassage: You say 'OK' when you agree with something. You also say it when something is good or fine.

old.definition: People who have lived for many, many years. Not young people.
old.exampleSentence: The old sit in the park every day.
old.contextPassage: We say 'the old' to talk about all old people together. Old people have lived a long, long time.
distractors: People who are very young and have not lived long.
People who are sick and need help from a doctor.
People who work hard and are very strong and fast.

ok.definition: A word you say when you agree or when something is good.
ok.exampleSentence: Is this seat ok for you?
ok.contextPassage: You can say ok when you are happy with something. People also say ok to show they understand.

on.definition: You do this to make something start working. You press a button and it starts.
on.exampleSentence: Please on the light in the room.
on.contextPassage: You can on the TV to watch a show. You on the radio to hear music.
distractors: To make something stop working. You press a button and it stops.
To make something very loud. The sound is very big.
To break something so it does not work.

once.definition: Something happens one time. It does not happen two times or more.
once.exampleSentence: I ate pizza once this week.
once.contextPassage: You can do something once and then stop. Once means only one time, not again.
distractors: Something happens two times. It happens again after the first time.
Something happens every day. It does not stop.
Something happens very fast. It is quick and then slow.

one number/det.,.definition: The number 1. It is the first number. It is not two or three.
one number/det.,.exampleSentence: I have one apple in my bag.
one number/det.,.contextPassage: One means a single thing. You use it to count the first thing.
distractors: The number 2. You have two things, not one.
No things at all. You have nothing.
A very big number. You cannot count it.

onion.definition: A round vegetable. It is white or yellow inside. It can make your eyes wet when you cut it.
onion.exampleSentence: My mum puts onion in the soup.
onion.contextPassage: An onion has a strong smell. People use it to cook many foods.
distractors: A small red fruit. It is sweet and you can eat it in summer.
A long orange vegetable. It grows in the ground and is sweet.
A green vegetable with many small parts. You can eat it in a salad.

online.definition: You make something ready and it starts to work on the internet.
online.exampleSentence: The teacher goes online to start the class.
online.contextPassage: You can go online with a computer or phone. When you are online you can see websites.
distractors: You turn off your computer. It does not work now.
You write a letter and send it in the post. It goes to a person far away.
You call a person on the phone. You talk to them with your voice.

only.definition: A child with no brothers and no sisters. This child is alone in the family.
only.exampleSentence: She is an only child at home.
only.contextPassage: An only child has no siblings. This child plays alone or with friends.
distractors: A child who has one brother. They play and eat together at home.
A child who is very tall. This child is the biggest in the school.
A child who is very good at school. This child gets good marks.

open.definition: Something is not closed. You can go in or look inside.
open.exampleSentence: The door is open, so come in.
open.contextPassage: A window can be open on a hot day. When a shop is open you can go inside.
distractors: Something is shut. You cannot go in or look inside.
Something is very big. It has a lot of space inside.
Something is broken. It does not work now.

or.definition: You use this word to show two choices. You can pick one thing or the other thing.
or.exampleSentence: Do you want tea or water?
or.contextPassage: You say 'this or that' when you have two things to choose. Only one can be the answer.
distractors: You use this word to join two things that are the same. Both things are true together.
You use this word to say something is not true. The answer is no.
You use this word to show why something happens. It gives the reason.

orange.definition: A round fruit. It is orange in colour. It is sweet and you can drink its juice.
orange.exampleSentence: I eat one orange every morning.
orange.contextPassage: An orange grows on a tree. You peel it to eat the soft inside.
distractors: A small red or green fruit. It grows on a tree and is very sweet.
A long yellow fruit. It is soft and easy to eat.
A small round fruit. It is red inside and very sweet in summer.

order.definition: Things are in the right place. First, then second, then third. Everything is in a line.
order.exampleSentence: The books are in order on the shelf.
order.contextPassage: You put things in order so it is easy to find them. Numbers and letters can be in order.
distractors: Things are in a big pile. They are not tidy or in a line.
One big thing. It is heavy and hard to move.
A colour or shape. You can see it with your eyes.

other.definition: A different person or thing. It is not this one. It is not me or you.
other.exampleSentence: One girl is tall and the other is small.
other.contextPassage: You use 'other' to talk about a different thing. It is not the same one you talked about before.
distractors: The same person or thing as before. It is not new or different.
A person you know very well. You see them every day.
The first thing in a group. It comes before all the rest.

our.definition: This word means it belongs to us. You and me have it together.
our.exampleSentence: This is our house. We live here.
our.contextPassage: You say 'our' when something belongs to a group with you in it. Our school, our food, our friends.
distractors: This word means it belongs to one person. It is not mine.
This word means it belongs to them. It is not with me or you.
This word means you do not have it. It is gone.

out.exampleSentence: She went out to buy food.
out.contextPassage: You use 'out' to say someone left a place. It is the opposite of 'in'.

outside.definition: Not inside a building; in the open air.
outside.exampleSentence: The children play outside every day.
outside.contextPassage: You go outside to be in the open air. It is the opposite of 'inside'.

over.definition: Above something or on top of it.
over.exampleSentence: The bird flies over the house.
over.contextPassage: You use 'over' to say something is above or across another thing. The cat jumped over the wall.

own.definition: Belonging to you and no one else.
own.contextPassage: You use 'own' to say something belongs to you. It shows the thing is yours and not someone else's.

page.definition: One flat piece of paper in a book.
page.exampleSentence: Open your book to page ten.
page.contextPassage: A book has many pages. You turn a page to read more.

paint.definition: A wet liquid you put on a wall or paper to add color.
paint.exampleSentence: The paint on the wall is red.
paint.contextPassage: Paint can be many colors. It is wet first and then it gets dry.

painting.definition: Putting color on something with a brush.
painting.exampleSentence: She is painting the wall white.
painting.contextPassage: You use a brush for painting. You can paint a wall or a picture.

pair.exampleSentence: I have a new pair of shoes.
pair.contextPassage: You say 'a pair of' for two things that belong together. Socks and shoes come in pairs.

paper.definition: A thin flat sheet you write or draw on.
paper.exampleSentence: He writes his name on paper.
paper.contextPassage: Books and notebooks are made of paper. You can write, draw, or print on paper.

parent.exampleSentence: My parent is at home now.
parent.contextPassage: You have two parents: a mother and a father. Parents take care of their children.

park.definition: A big open green area where people can play and walk.
park.exampleSentence: We go to the park on Saturday.
park.contextPassage: A park has grass and trees. Children like to play in the park.

part.exampleSentence: This part of the book is good.
part.contextPassage: A car has many parts. Each part is one piece of the whole thing.

partner.definition: A person you work or do things with together.
partner.exampleSentence: She is my partner at work.
partner.contextPassage: A partner helps you. You do things together.

party.definition: One person or group in an agreement or deal.
party.exampleSentence: Both parties say yes to the deal.
party.contextPassage: Two parties can make an agreement. Each party has a role.

passport.definition: A small book from your country. It shows who you are when you travel.
passport.exampleSentence: I need my passport at the airport.
passport.contextPassage: You use a passport to go to other countries. It has your name and photo in it.

pay.definition: Money you get for doing your job.
pay.exampleSentence: He gets good pay at his new job.
pay.contextPassage: Pay comes every month for many people. You use your pay to buy food and things.

pen.definition: A small closed area where animals live on a farm.
pen.exampleSentence: The sheep are in the pen today.
pen.contextPassage: A pen keeps animals safe. Farmers use pens on their farms.

pencil.definition: A thin stick you use to paint pictures. It has color on the tip.
pencil.exampleSentence: She uses a pencil to paint the flowers.
pencil.contextPassage: A pencil has color at the end. You use it to make pictures.

people.definition: More than one person. Many people together.
people.exampleSentence: Many people are in the park today.
people.contextPassage: People can be big groups or small groups. We see people every day.

pepper.definition: A small green, red, or yellow food. You can eat it.
pepper.exampleSentence: I eat a red pepper with my lunch.
pepper.contextPassage: Pepper is a vegetable. It can be sweet or hot.

person.definition: One human being. One man, woman, or child.
person.exampleSentence: One person is at the door.
person.contextPassage: A person can be a man or a woman. Every person has a name.

phone.definition: A small thing you use to talk to people far away.
phone.exampleSentence: I call my mom on my phone.
phone.contextPassage: You can talk and send messages on a phone. Many people have a phone every day.

photo.definition: A picture you take with a camera.
photo.exampleSentence: I take a photo of my dog.
photo.contextPassage: A photo shows people or things. You can look at old photos to see the past.

photograph.definition: A picture made by a camera. It shows real things.
photograph.exampleSentence: This photograph is of my family.
photograph.contextPassage: A photograph can be on paper or on a phone. You take a photograph with a camera.

piano.definition: A big musical instrument with black and white keys. You press the keys to make music.
piano.exampleSentence: She plays the piano at school.
piano.contextPassage: A piano is very big and heavy. You can hear it in music.

picture.definition: A drawing or photo of something or someone. You can see it on paper or a wall.
picture.exampleSentence: I have a picture of my cat.
picture.contextPassage: You can draw a picture with a pencil. A picture can show a person or a place.

piece.definition: One part of something bigger. It is not the whole thing.
piece.exampleSentence: Can I have a piece of cake?
piece.contextPassage: You can cut food into small pieces. A piece of paper is one sheet.

pig.definition: A fat pink farm animal. It makes a loud noise and eats a lot.
pig.exampleSentence: The pig is on the farm.
pig.contextPassage: A pig has a short tail and a big nose. Farmers keep pigs on their land.

pink.definition: A light red color. It looks like red and white mixed together.
pink.exampleSentence: Her shirt is pink and pretty.
pink.contextPassage: Pink is a soft, light color. Many flowers are pink.

place.definition: A spot or area where something or someone is. It can be inside or outside.
place.exampleSentence: This is a good place to sit.
place.contextPassage: A park is a nice place to play. You can find a place on a map.

plan.definition: An idea about what you want to do. You think about it before you start.
plan.exampleSentence: We have a plan for the trip.
plan.contextPassage: A good plan helps you do things well. You can make a plan with your friends.

plane.definition: A big machine that flies in the sky. It takes people to far places.
plane.exampleSentence: The plane is big and fast.
plane.contextPassage: You go to an airport to get on a plane. A plane flies very high in the sky.

plant.definition: A living thing that is not an animal. It grows in soil and needs water and sun.
plant.exampleSentence: This plant has big green leaves.
plant.contextPassage: You can grow a plant at home. Plants need water every day.

play.definition: Fun things you do when you are not working. You do it because it is fun.
play.exampleSentence: The children love play in the park.
play.contextPassage: Play is good for children. Kids laugh and run during play.

player.definition: A person who plays a game or sport. This person is part of a team or game.
player.exampleSentence: She is a good football player.
player.contextPassage: A player needs to know the rules. Good players help their team.

please.definition: You say this word to be polite. You use it when you ask for something.
please.contextPassage: Say please when you want something. It is a polite and kind word.

police.definition: A group of people. They keep everyone safe. They stop bad things.
police.exampleSentence: The police help people in the city.
police.contextPassage: You can call the police when you need help. They wear a uniform.

policeman.definition: A man who works in the police. He keeps people safe.
policeman.exampleSentence: The policeman stands near the school.
policeman.contextPassage: A policeman wears a uniform every day. He helps people on the street.

pool.definition: A small area of water. You can swim in it.
pool.exampleSentence: The children swim in the pool.
pool.contextPassage: A pool has cold water in it. It can be big or small.

poor.definition: People who do not have money. They cannot buy many things.
poor.exampleSentence: The poor need food and help.
poor.contextPassage: Some people help the poor. They give food and clothes to them.

post.definition: A long piece of wood or metal. It stands in the ground.
post.exampleSentence: There is a post near the gate.
post.contextPassage: A post can hold a fence. You can see posts on the street too.

potato.definition: A food that grows in the ground. It is brown outside and white inside.
potato.exampleSentence: I eat a potato with my dinner.
potato.contextPassage: A potato is a very common food. You can cook it in hot water.

pound.definition: A unit to measure how heavy something is. It is used in some countries.
pound.exampleSentence: This bag is five pounds heavy.
pound.contextPassage: People use pounds to say how heavy food is. One pound is not very big.

prefer.definition: To like one thing more than another thing.
prefer.contextPassage: You can prefer one food or drink. It is the thing you like more.

pretty.definition: A person who looks very nice. People like to see them.
pretty.exampleSentence: She is a pretty girl in my class.
pretty.contextPassage: You can say a person is pretty. It means they look very nice.

price.definition: How much money you pay for something.
price.exampleSentence: The price of this apple is one dollar.
price.contextPassage: You can see the price in a shop. A big price means you pay a lot of money.

problem.definition: Something that is hard or bad. You need to fix it.
problem.exampleSentence: I have a big problem with my car.
problem.contextPassage: A problem is not good. You need to find an answer for it.

programme.definition: A set of things you do one after another. It has a plan.
programme.exampleSentence: We have a new programme at school.
programme.contextPassage: A programme tells you what to do. You can have a programme every day.

purple.definition: A colour. It is a mix of red and blue.
purple.contextPassage: Purple is a dark colour. Some flowers are purple.

put.definition: A right to sell something at a set price. This is a money word.
put.exampleSentence: He has a put on those shares.
put.contextPassage: A put is used in money and trading. This word is not common for A1 learners.

quarter.definition: One part of four equal parts. Four quarters make one whole.
quarter.exampleSentence: I eat one quarter of the apple.
quarter.contextPassage: You can cut a pizza into quarters. Each quarter is the same size.

question.definition: Words you say or write to get an answer. You ask a question.
question.contextPassage: A question ends with a question mark. You ask questions to learn new things.

quick.definition: The soft skin under your nails. It can hurt when you touch it.
quick.exampleSentence: She cut her nail to the quick.
quick.contextPassage: The quick is very soft and pink. It hurts a lot if you cut it.

quickly.definition: You do something fast. You do not go slow.
quickly.exampleSentence: He runs quickly to the bus.
quickly.contextPassage: She eats quickly because she is late. The dog runs quickly in the park.

quiet.definition: No sound. It is very still and silent.
quiet.exampleSentence: The room is very quiet now.
quiet.contextPassage: At night it is quiet outside. I like quiet when I read.

radio.definition: A thing that plays music and voices. It gets sounds from the air.
radio.exampleSentence: I listen to the radio in the morning.
radio.contextPassage: You can hear music on the radio. My mum turns on the radio in the car.

rain.definition: Water that falls from the sky. It comes from clouds.
rain.exampleSentence: There is a lot of rain today.
rain.contextPassage: Rain makes the ground wet. I need an umbrella when it rains.

read.definition: A time when you look at words. You look at a book or text.
read.exampleSentence: I had a good read this afternoon.
read.contextPassage: A read is a time you spend with a book. It is a nice thing to do.

reading.definition: You look at words and understand them. You do this with a book.
reading.exampleSentence: She is reading a book now.
reading.contextPassage: I like reading in bed at night. Reading helps you learn new words.

ready.definition: You can start now. Everything is good to go.
ready.exampleSentence: I am ready for school now.
ready.contextPassage: Are you ready? We can go when you are ready.

real.definition: Something is real when it is true and not made up.
real.exampleSentence: This is a real dog, not a toy.
real.contextPassage: You can touch and see real things. A real apple is not a picture.

really.definition: You say really to show something is very true or very big.
really.contextPassage: Really makes a word stronger. This bag is really heavy.

red.definition: Red is a color. It is the color of blood and fire.
red.exampleSentence: I have a red apple in my bag.
red.contextPassage: Red is a bright color. Stop signs are red.

relax.definition: To relax is to rest and feel calm and good.
relax.exampleSentence: I sit down and relax after school.
relax.contextPassage: You can relax on a sofa. It is good to relax every day.

remember.definition: To remember is to think of something from before in your head.
remember.exampleSentence: I remember my first day at school.
remember.contextPassage: You can remember a name or a place. It is good to remember your friends.

repeat.definition: A repeat is when something happens again, one more time.
repeat.exampleSentence: This song is a repeat from yesterday.
repeat.contextPassage: A repeat is the same thing again. We watch a repeat on TV.

restaurant.definition: A restaurant is a place where you go to eat food. People bring the food to you.
restaurant.exampleSentence: We eat dinner at a restaurant today.
restaurant.contextPassage: A restaurant has tables and chairs. You can eat good food there.

rice.definition: Rice is a small white food. You cook it in water and eat it.
rice.exampleSentence: I eat rice and vegetables for lunch.
rice.contextPassage: Rice is a very common food. Many people eat rice every day.

rich.definition: To make something better and give it more good things.
rich.exampleSentence: Good food can rich your body with energy.
rich.contextPassage: You can rich a meal with vegetables. More color and food rich your plate.

ride.definition: A ride is when you go somewhere on a bike, car, or horse.
ride.exampleSentence: I have a ride on my bike today.
ride.contextPassage: A ride can be short or long. I love a fast ride on my bike.

right.definition: Something right is not bent. It goes in one line, not to the side.
right.exampleSentence: Draw a right line on the paper.
right.contextPassage: A right line does not go up or down. It is flat and not bent.

river.definition: A river is a long line of water. It moves and goes to the sea.
river.exampleSentence: We see a big river near our town.
river.contextPassage: A river has water that moves. Fish live in the river.

road.definition: A long path where cars and trucks go.
road.exampleSentence: The car goes fast on the road.
road.contextPassage: A road connects two places. You walk or drive on a road.

room.definition: A space where something can go or fit.
room.exampleSentence: There is no room for my bag here.
room.contextPassage: When a place is full, there is no room. When a place is big, there is a lot of room.

rule.definition: A thing you must do or not do.
rule.exampleSentence: The rule says no running in school.
rule.contextPassage: Rules help people know what to do. A school has many rules.

run.definition: To move your legs very fast to go somewhere.
run.exampleSentence: The children run in the park.
run.contextPassage: You run when you are late. Dogs also like to run and play.

sad.definition: To make someone feel not happy.
sad.exampleSentence: The bad news makes her sad.
sad.contextPassage: A sad film can make you cry. You feel sad when something bad happens.

salad.definition: A cold food made of vegetables like tomatoes and lettuce.
salad.exampleSentence: I eat a salad for lunch today.
salad.contextPassage: A salad is cold and good for you. You can put oil on your salad.

salt.definition: A white powder you put on food to make it taste good.
salt.exampleSentence: He puts salt on his eggs.
salt.contextPassage: Salt is small and white. You find salt on the table in a restaurant.

same.definition: Not different; equal to another thing.
same.exampleSentence: We have the same red bag.
same.contextPassage: These two apples look the same. My shoes and your shoes are the same.

sandwich.definition: Two pieces of bread with food inside, like cheese or meat.
sandwich.exampleSentence: She eats a cheese sandwich for lunch.
sandwich.contextPassage: A sandwich is easy to make. You can put chicken or vegetables in a sandwich.

saturday.definition: A day of the week. It comes after Friday.
saturday.exampleSentence: We go to the park on Saturday.
saturday.contextPassage: Saturday is not a school day. Many people stay home and play on Saturday.

say.definition: A turn to speak and give your idea.
say.exampleSentence: I want a say in this plan.
say.contextPassage: Everyone can have a say. You have a say when people listen to you.

school.definition: A big group of fish that swim together.
school.exampleSentence: A school of fish swims in the sea.
school.contextPassage: Fish swim together in a school. A school of fish can be very big.

science.definition: Science is how we learn about the world. We look, test, and find answers.
science.contextPassage: In science, we ask questions and find answers. We can study animals, water, or the sky.

sea.definition: The sea is a very big area of salt water. Fish live in it.
sea.exampleSentence: I can see the sea from here.
sea.contextPassage: The sea is very big and blue. Boats go on the sea.

second.definition: Second is the number two in a list. It comes after first.
second.exampleSentence: I am second in the line.
second.contextPassage: First, second, third — these are numbers in order. She is second today, not first.

see.definition: To see is to look at something with your eyes. You use your eyes to see.
see.exampleSentence: I can see a big dog.
see.contextPassage: We see things with our eyes. I can see the sun today.

sell.definition: To sell is to give something to a person for money. They pay you and you give it to them.
sell.exampleSentence: She wants to sell her old bike.
sell.contextPassage: You can sell things at a shop. He sells fruit every day.

send.definition: To send is to make something go to another person. You do not take it — it goes to them.
send.exampleSentence: I want to send this to my friend.
send.contextPassage: You can send a letter or a message. She sends a card to her mum.

sentence.definition: A sentence is words put together to say something. It has a start and an end.
sentence.exampleSentence: I can read this sentence now.
sentence.contextPassage: A sentence starts with a big letter. It ends with a dot or a mark.

september.definition: September is the ninth month of the year. It comes after August.
september.contextPassage: September comes after August. School often starts in September.

seven number.definition: Seven is a number. It comes after six and before eight.
seven number.exampleSentence: I have seven apples in my bag.
seven number.contextPassage: Six, seven, eight — these are numbers. Seven is not a big number.

seventeen number.definition: Seventeen is a number. It is ten and seven together.
seventeen number.exampleSentence: She has seventeen books at home.
seventeen number.contextPassage: Seventeen comes after sixteen. It is more than ten but less than twenty.

seventy number.definition: Seventy is a big number. It is seven times ten.
seventy number.exampleSentence: My grandpa is seventy years old.
seventy number.contextPassage: Seventy comes after sixty. It is less than one hundred.

she.definition: She is a word for a girl or a woman. We use it when we talk about her.
she.contextPassage: We say she when we talk about a girl or woman. She is not here today.

sheep.definition: A farm animal with thick, soft fur. People use it for wool and meat.
sheep.exampleSentence: I see many sheep on the farm.
sheep.contextPassage: Sheep eat grass. A baby sheep is called a lamb.

shirt.definition: A piece of clothing you wear on the top part of your body.
shirt.exampleSentence: I wear a blue shirt today.
shirt.contextPassage: A shirt has buttons or no buttons. You put it on over your head or from the front.

shoe.definition: You put a shoe on your foot. It keeps your foot safe and warm.
shoe.exampleSentence: I have a new shoe on my foot.
shoe.contextPassage: You wear two shoes, one on each foot. Shoes are not as tall as boots.

shop.definition: A place where you can go and buy things.
shop.exampleSentence: My mom goes to the shop every day.
shop.contextPassage: A shop sells things like food or clothes. You give money and get the thing you want.

shopping.definition: When you go to shops to look at and buy things.
shopping.exampleSentence: I go shopping with my mom on Saturday.
shopping.contextPassage: You can do shopping at a big store or a small shop. Many people like shopping for new clothes.

short.definition: Not tall or not long. Small from top to bottom or end to end.
short.exampleSentence: This boy is very short and small.
short.contextPassage: A short person is not tall. A short road does not go very far.

should.definition: A word that tells you something is a good idea to do.
should.exampleSentence: You should eat good food every day.
should.contextPassage: We use 'should' to give advice. For example, you should go to bed early.

show.definition: A fun thing to watch, like a play or a dance on a stage.
show.exampleSentence: We see a great show at school today.
show.contextPassage: A show can have singing, dancing, or acting. People sit and watch the show together.

shower.definition: A short time when rain falls from the sky.
shower.exampleSentence: There is a small shower outside right now.
shower.contextPassage: A shower does not last a long time. After a shower, the ground is wet.

sick.definition: When your body does not feel good, you are sick.
sick.exampleSentence: My friend is sick and stays at home.
sick.contextPassage: A sick person needs rest and water. You can go to a doctor when you are sick.

sing.definition: To make music with your voice and words.
sing.exampleSentence: I sing a song with my friends.
sing.contextPassage: You can sing in a group or alone. Many people sing happy songs together.

singer.definition: A person who sings. It is their job to sing songs.
singer.exampleSentence: The singer has a very good voice.
singer.contextPassage: A singer performs songs for people to hear. Some singers sing on a big stage.

sister.definition: A girl or woman who has the same mother and father as you.
sister.exampleSentence: My sister has long brown hair.
sister.contextPassage: You can say 'big sister' or 'little sister'. A sister is a girl in your family.

sit.definition: To put your body down on a chair or the floor.
sit.exampleSentence: Please sit on the chair here.
sit.contextPassage: You sit when you are at school or at dinner. You can sit on a chair, a bed, or the floor.

six number.definition: The number 6. It comes after five and before seven.
six number.exampleSentence: I have six apples in my bag.
six number.contextPassage: You can count to six on your fingers. Six plus one is seven.

sixteen number.definition: The number 16. It comes after fifteen and before seventeen.
sixteen number.exampleSentence: There are sixteen children in the class.
sixteen number.contextPassage: Ten plus six is sixteen. You can write it as 16.

sixty number.definition: The number 60. It comes after fifty-nine and before sixty-one.
sixty number.exampleSentence: There are sixty minutes in one hour.
sixty number.contextPassage: Six times ten is sixty. You can write it as 60.

skirt.definition: A piece of clothing that hangs from the waist down. Girls and women wear it.
skirt.exampleSentence: She has a blue skirt today.
skirt.contextPassage: A skirt can be long or short. You wear a skirt on the bottom part of your body.

sleep.definition: To close your eyes and rest your body at night.
sleep.exampleSentence: I sleep in my bed every night.
sleep.contextPassage: People sleep when they are tired. You go to sleep in a bed.

slow.definition: Not fast. Something slow does not move quickly.
slow.exampleSentence: The old cat is very slow today.
slow.contextPassage: A slow car does not go fast. You can say 'slow down' to a person or a car.

small.definition: Not big. A small thing is little in size.
small.exampleSentence: This dog is very small and cute.
small.contextPassage: A small bag cannot hold many things. The opposite of small is big.

snake.definition: A long, thin animal with no legs. It moves on the ground.
snake.exampleSentence: I see a big snake in the garden.
snake.contextPassage: A snake has no arms or legs. Some snakes are dangerous and some are not.

snow.definition: Soft white pieces of frozen water that fall from the sky in winter.
snow.exampleSentence: There is snow on the ground today.
snow.contextPassage: Snow is cold and white. Children like to play in the snow.

so.definition: Very or in this way. You use it to make a word stronger.
so.contextPassage: You can say 'so big' or 'so happy'. It helps you show a strong feeling.

some.definition: A number that is not exact, but close to the real number.
some.exampleSentence: The shop is some ten minutes from here.
some.contextPassage: We use 'some' when a number is not exact. It means about or around that number.

somebody.definition: A person, but we do not say who the person is.
somebody.contextPassage: We say 'somebody' when we do not know the person. It means one person, but not a named person.

someone.definition: A person, but we do not say who the person is.
someone.contextPassage: 'Someone' and 'somebody' mean the same thing. We use them when we do not know who the person is.

something.definition: A thing, but we do not say what the thing is.
something.exampleSentence: I want to eat something now.
something.contextPassage: We say 'something' when we do not name the thing. It is one thing, but we do not know or say what it is.

sometimes.definition: Not always, but more than one time.
sometimes.exampleSentence: I sometimes eat soup for lunch.
sometimes.contextPassage: We use 'sometimes' to say a thing does not happen every day. It happens a few times, but not always.

son.definition: A boy or man is the son of his mother and father.
son.exampleSentence: Their son is five years old.
son.contextPassage: A son is a male child. A mother and father can have a son and a daughter.

song.definition: Words and music together that you sing.
song.exampleSentence: She sings a song every morning.
song.contextPassage: A song has words and a tune. You can sing a song or listen to a song.

soon.definition: A short time from now, not a long wait.
soon.contextPassage: We say 'soon' when something will happen in a little time. It will not take long.

sorry.definition: What you say when you do a bad thing to someone.
sorry.exampleSentence: He said sorry to his friend.
sorry.contextPassage: You say sorry when you do something wrong. It shows you feel bad about it.

sound.definition: Good and strong, with no problems or sickness.
sound.exampleSentence: The doctor says he is sound.
sound.contextPassage: We say someone is sound when they are well and healthy. It means nothing is wrong with them.

soup.definition: A hot food made with water and things like meat or vegetables.
soup.exampleSentence: I eat hot soup for dinner.
soup.contextPassage: Soup is a liquid food. You can make it with vegetables, meat, or water.

south.definition: One of the four big directions, the opposite of north.
south.exampleSentence: Spain is in the south of Europe.
south.contextPassage: South is a direction. On a map, south is at the bottom.

speak.definition: A small, cheap bar where people drink.
speak.exampleSentence: He goes to the old speak every night.
speak.contextPassage: A speak is a small, dark bar. People go there to drink cheap drinks.

special.definition: A lower price on something for a short time.
special.exampleSentence: The shop has a special on apples today.
special.contextPassage: A special means you pay less money. It is not always there, only for a short time.

spell.definition: Magic words that do something to a person.
spell.exampleSentence: The witch says a spell on him.
spell.contextPassage: A spell is magic words. People in stories use spells to do good or bad things.

spelling.definition: To use a spell to control someone with magic.
spelling.exampleSentence: The witch is spelling the boy in the story.
spelling.contextPassage: Spelling someone means using magic on them. It can make them do or feel things.

spend.definition: The amount of money you use to buy things.
spend.exampleSentence: My spend at the shop is ten dollars.
spend.contextPassage: Your spend is all the money you pay. A big spend means you buy a lot of things.

sport.definition: A game where you use your body and follow rules.
sport.contextPassage: In a sport, you play and try to win. You use your body and follow the rules.

spring.definition: To hurt a part of your body by pulling it too hard.
spring.exampleSentence: She can spring her leg if she runs fast.
spring.contextPassage: You can spring a leg or an arm. It hurts and you need to rest.

stand.definition: The act of being up on your feet.
stand.exampleSentence: His stand on the bus is very long.
stand.contextPassage: A stand means you are up on your feet. You do not sit down.

star.definition: A small bright light you see in the sky at night.
star.exampleSentence: I can see one big star tonight.
star.contextPassage: Stars are in the sky at night. They look small but they are very big and far away.

start.exampleSentence: The start of the game is at two.
start.contextPassage: The start is when something first begins. After the start, you are in the middle.

tomato.definition: A round, red food that grows on a plant.
tomato.exampleSentence: I eat a red tomato for lunch.
tomato.contextPassage: A tomato is red and soft inside. You can eat it in a salad or in a sauce.

tomorrow.definition: The day that comes after today.
tomorrow.contextPassage: Tomorrow is not today. It is the next day that comes after today.

tonight.definition: The night of today. Not yesterday. Not tomorrow.
tonight.contextPassage: Tonight means this night, right now. You can say: I go to bed tonight.
distractors: The morning of today. The sun is up.
The day before today. It is not now.
The morning of the next day. It is not here yet.

too.definition: Also. The same for me or for another person.
too.exampleSentence: I like cats and I like dogs too.
too.contextPassage: You use too to say the same thing is true for someone else. She is hungry and he is hungry too.
distractors: Only this one thing. Not another.
Very much. A big amount of something.
Not this. The other one.

tooth.definition: A small, hard, white thing in your mouth. You use it to eat food.
tooth.exampleSentence: I brush my tooth every morning.
tooth.contextPassage: You have many teeth in your mouth. Teeth are hard and white.
distractors: The long, soft thing in your mouth. You use it to taste food.
The two soft things on your face. You use them to eat and talk.
The hard thing at the top of your mouth. It is not a tooth.

tourist.definition: A person who goes to a new place for fun. Not for work.
tourist.exampleSentence: The tourist takes a photo of the big church.
tourist.contextPassage: A tourist visits new cities and sees new things. Tourists often have a camera and a map.
distractors: A person who goes to a new place to work every day.
A person who shows other people around a city. It is their job.
A person who drives people to the airport in a car.

town.definition: A place where many people live. It is bigger than a village but smaller than a city.
town.exampleSentence: My family lives in a small town.
town.contextPassage: A town has shops and houses. Many people live and work in a town.
distractors: A very big place where millions of people live and work.
A very small place where only a few people live.
A place outside the town where there are trees and animals.

traffic.definition: Many cars and buses on the road at one time.
traffic.exampleSentence: There is a lot of traffic on this road today.
traffic.contextPassage: Traffic can be slow or fast. In the morning there is often a lot of traffic.
distractors: A place where cars stop and people buy food and water.
The sound a car makes when it goes very fast.
A big road where many cars go very fast.

train.definition: A big, long vehicle. It moves on metal tracks and carries many people.
train.exampleSentence: I go to school by train every day.
train.contextPassage: A train moves on rails. You can go to many places by train.
distractors: A big vehicle that flies in the sky and carries people.
A big vehicle on water. It carries people and things.
A small vehicle with two wheels. You use your legs to move it.

travel.definition: Going from one place to another place. You can go far or near.
travel.exampleSentence: I love travel to new countries.
travel.contextPassage: Travel can be by car, train, or plane. Many people travel for fun.
distractors: Staying in one place for a long time. You do not move.
Going back to the same place every day. It is your home.
Carrying big and heavy things from one room to another.

tree.definition: A very tall plant. It has a big brown trunk and many green leaves.
tree.exampleSentence: There is a big tree in our garden.
tree.contextPassage: Trees are tall and green. Birds live in trees.
distractors: A small, green plant. It grows in the ground and is not tall.
A plant with many flowers. It smells very good.
A plant that grows in water. It is green and very small.

trip.definition: A short journey to a place. You go and then come back.
trip.exampleSentence: We go on a trip to the beach today.
trip.contextPassage: A trip can be one day or many days. You go to a new place on a trip.
distractors: Staying at home for a long time. You do not go anywhere.
A long time when you stop work and stay at home.
Going to work every morning and coming home at night.

trousers.definition: A piece of clothing for your legs. It covers both legs and your waist.
trousers.exampleSentence: He wears blue trousers to school.
trousers.contextPassage: Trousers cover your legs. You put them on every morning.
distractors: A piece of clothing you put on your top. It covers your arms and body.
A short piece of clothing. It covers only the top part of your legs.
A piece of clothing for a woman. It is long and covers the legs.

true.definition: Something that is real and correct. It is not a lie.
true.exampleSentence: It is true that cats drink milk.
true.contextPassage: You say true when something is right. The opposite of true is false.
distractors: Something that is not real. It is a lie or a story.
Something that you are not sure about. You do not know if it is right.
Something that is possible. Maybe it is right or maybe not.

try.definition: When you do something to see if you can do it.
try.exampleSentence: I try to eat new food today.
try.contextPassage: You try when something is not easy. It is good to try new things.
distractors: When you stop doing something.
When you do something very well.
When you ask someone for help.

t-shirt.definition: A soft top with short arms and no buttons.

t-shirt.contextPassage: A t-shirt is easy to wear. Many t-shirts have a picture on them.
distractors: A warm top with long arms and buttons.
A top you wear only in cold weather.
A long piece of cloth you wear on your legs.

tuesday.definition: The day after Monday. It is the second day of the week.
tuesday.exampleSentence: I go to school on Tuesday.
tuesday.contextPassage: Tuesday comes after Monday. It comes before Wednesday.
distractors: The day after Sunday. It is the first day of the week.
The day after Tuesday. It is the third day of the week.
The day after Thursday. It is the fifth day of the week.

turn.definition: To move your body or a thing to face a new way.
turn.exampleSentence: Turn left at the big school.
turn.contextPassage: You can turn your head to look at something. You can turn a car to go a new way.
distractors: To move fast from one place to another.
To stop and stay in one place.
To pick something up from the floor.

tv.definition: A big screen in your home. You watch shows and films on it.
tv.exampleSentence: I watch a film on the tv.
tv.contextPassage: A tv is in the living room. You can watch it with your family.
distractors: A small box you use to listen to music.
A screen you use to write and send messages.
A flat thing you read books and news on.

twelve number.definition: The number 12. It comes after eleven.

twelve number.contextPassage: Twelve comes after eleven. One year has twelve months.
distractors: The number 10. It comes after nine.
The number 11. It comes after ten.
The number 13. It comes after twelve.

twenty number.definition: The number 20. It comes after nineteen.

twenty number.contextPassage: Twenty comes after nineteen. Two times ten is twenty.
distractors: The number 10. It is two times five.
The number 12. It comes after eleven.
The number 30. It comes after twenty-nine.

twice.exampleSentence: I brush my teeth twice every day.
twice.contextPassage: Twice means two times. I eat twice a day.
distractors: One time. You do something and then stop.
Three times. You do something, stop, and do it two more times.
Many times. You do something again and again and again.

two number.definition: The number 2. It comes after one.
two number.exampleSentence: I have two cats at home.
two number.contextPassage: Two comes after one. One plus one is two.
distractors: The number 1. It comes before two.
The number 3. It comes after two.
The number 4. It comes after three.

umbrella.definition: A thing you hold over your head when it rains. It keeps you dry.
umbrella.exampleSentence: I take my umbrella because it rains today.
umbrella.contextPassage: An umbrella is very useful in bad weather. You open it when it rains.
distractors: A warm thing you wear on your body in cold weather.
A big hat you wear on your head in hot weather.
A long stick you hold to help you walk.

uncle.definition: The brother of your mum or dad.
uncle.exampleSentence: My uncle has a big red car.
uncle.contextPassage: Your uncle is family. He is the brother of your mum or dad.
distractors: The father of your mum or dad.
The son of your mum and dad.
The husband of your mum.

under.definition: Below something. It is lower than something else.

under.contextPassage: Under means below something. The shoes are under the bed.
distractors: Next to something. It is not above or below.
On top of something. It is higher than something else.
Far away from something. It is not close.

understand.contextPassage: You understand when something is clear to you. It is good to understand new words.
distractors: To say words out loud to other people.
To write words on a paper.
To listen to a sound but not know it.

university.definition: A big school for big students. You go there to learn a lot about one thing.
university.exampleSentence: My sister goes to university every day.
university.contextPassage: Students study at university for many years. You go to university after school.
distractors: A small school for very young children.
A place where sick people go to get help.
A place where people go to buy books.

until.definition: Up to a time. It stops at that time.
until.exampleSentence: I wait here until you come back.
until.contextPassage: You can say 'until' to show when something stops. I play until it is dark.
distractors: A long time after something happens.
At the same time as something else.
Before something starts to happen.

up.definition: The way that goes away from the ground. It is not down.
up.exampleSentence: The bird goes up in the sky.
up.contextPassage: Up is the way above you. You look up to see the sky.
distractors: The way that goes to the left side.
The way that goes behind you.
The way that goes to the ground.

upstairs.definition: The part of a house that is above you. You go up steps to get there.

upstairs.contextPassage: You walk up the steps to go upstairs. The rooms upstairs are above the rooms downstairs.
distractors: The room you first walk into in a house.
The part of a house below the ground.
The small room outside a house.

us.definition: Me and one or more other people together.

us.contextPassage: You say 'us' when you talk about yourself and others. The teacher looks at us and smiles.
distractors: One person who is not you.
A person you do not know.
The person you are talking to right now.

use.definition: When you do something with an object to help you.
use.exampleSentence: The use of a pen is for writing.
use.contextPassage: Every object has a use. A cup has a use for drinking water.
distractors: When you buy a new thing from a shop.
When you break something by accident.
When you give a thing to another person.

useful.definition: Something that can help you do things. It is good to have.
useful.exampleSentence: A bag is very useful for school.
useful.contextPassage: Useful things make life easier. A pen is useful when you write.
distractors: Something that is very beautiful to look at.
Something that is very old and broken.
Something that is big but hard to carry.

usually.definition: Most days or most times. Not all the time, but very often.
usually.exampleSentence: I usually eat breakfast in the morning.
usually.contextPassage: You do something usually when you do it most days. She usually walks to school.
distractors: One time and then never again.
Not very often, only a few times.
Every single time without stopping.

vacation.definition: A time when you do not go to work or school. You can rest and have fun.
vacation.exampleSentence: We go to the beach on vacation.
vacation.contextPassage: People go on vacation to rest. You do not have school on vacation.
distractors: A day when you feel sick and stay in bed.
A short break to eat food in the middle of the day.
A big celebration with music and dancing.

vegetable.definition: A plant that people eat. It is good for you.
vegetable.exampleSentence: A carrot is a vegetable I eat every day.
vegetable.contextPassage: You can cook or eat vegetables fresh. Vegetables like tomatoes and carrots are good food.

A drink that comes from plants.
A food that comes from an animal.

very.definition: A word that makes another word stronger. It means a lot.

very.contextPassage: You use 'very' before words like big, small, or good. It shows something is more than normal.

A word you use to say no to something.
A word that means something happens one time.

video.definition: A moving picture you can watch on a screen.
video.exampleSentence: I watch a video on my phone.
video.contextPassage: You can see videos on a computer or TV. A video can be short or long.
distractors: A book you read with many pictures in it.
A game you play with your hands on a screen.
A song you listen to on the radio.

village.definition: A very small town with not many people.

village.contextPassage: A village has a few houses and streets. It is smaller than a town.
distractors: A very big place with many people and tall buildings.
A place where people grow food and keep animals.
A small house near a river or forest.

visit.definition: When you go to see a person or a place.
visit.exampleSentence: We had a nice visit to the park.
visit.contextPassage: A visit can be short or long. You can pay a visit to a friend or a new place.

When you write a letter to someone far away.
When you call someone on the phone to talk.

visitor.definition: A person who comes to see you or a place.

visitor.contextPassage: A visitor does not live in your home. They come and then go back to their own home.
distractors: A person who lives in your house with you every day.
A person who works in a shop and helps you buy things.
A person who teaches children in a school.

distractors: A time when you move very fast to get somewhere.
A time when you talk to many people in a big room.
A time when you eat food and drink at a table.

waiter.definition: A person who brings food to you in a restaurant.

waiter.contextPassage: You ask the waiter what food is good today. The waiter works in a café or restaurant.

A person who takes your money when you buy food at a shop.
A person who cleans the tables and floors in a restaurant.

wake.definition: When you stop sleeping and open your eyes.

wake.contextPassage: After a wake you get up from your bed. You can wake up when you hear a loud sound.

When you feel very tired and want to rest.
When you eat food in the morning after sleeping.

walk.definition: To move on your feet, one step at a time, not fast.

To sit on something and move from one place to another.

A flat thing on the floor that you walk on every day.

A feeling you get when you are very cold and need a coat.
A feeling you get when you are sad and start to cry.

warm.contextPassage: Warm water is not cold and not very hot. A warm day is nice to go outside.
distractors: Very hot and hard to touch or be near.
Very cold and not good for your hands or face.
Not hot and not cold, exactly in the middle.

wash.contextPassage: You have a wash to clean your hands or body. You can give your clothes a wash too.

When you use a brush to clean your teeth in the morning.
When you put clean clothes on your body after sleeping.

watch.definition: A small clock you wear on your arm.

watch.contextPassage: You look at a watch to see the time. You wear it on your wrist.
distractors: A small machine that takes photos.
A bag you carry in your hand.
A small light you wear at night.

water.definition: A clear liquid you drink. Rain and rivers have water.

distractors: A white liquid that comes from a cow.

A hot brown drink many people like in the morning.

way.definition: The road or path you go on to get to a place.

A short walk from your home to a shop.

we.definition: Me and one or more other people together.
we.exampleSentence: We go to school every day.
we.contextPassage: You say we when you talk about yourself and other people. We is used instead of all our names.
distractors: One person who is not you.
The person you are talking to.
One person far away from you.

wear.definition: Clothes that people put on their body.

wear.contextPassage: You can find wear for hot days and cold days. People choose wear for work or play.
distractors: A big bag you carry on your back.
A soft cover you put on your bed.
A small piece of cloth you use to clean your face.

weather.contextPassage: You look outside to see the weather. The weather can be sunny, rainy, or cloudy.
distractors: The time when the sun goes down at night.

The time of year like spring or winter.

A TV show you watch on a big screen.

The direction at the bottom of a map.

A word you use to ask about a time.

The reason why something happens.

The person who does something.

A word you use to ask about a thing.
A word you use to ask about a time.

A sweet drink made from milk and fruit.

with.definition: Together, or using something. You are not alone.

Before something or someone arrives.

Doing something at the same time as another thing.

A young child who is a girl, not yet grown up.

wonderful.definition: Very very good. It makes you very happy.

word.definition: A small unit of language. You read and say words.

A sound, like music, that you hear in a song.

A place where children go to study and learn.

world.definition: All the people and places on Earth. It is very big.

write.definition: To make letters or words on paper. You use a pen.

writer.definition: A person who makes books or stories with words.

writer.contextPassage: A writer uses words to tell stories. Many people like to read a writer's books.
distractors: A person who draws pictures in books.
A person who reads books to children.
A person who sells books in a shop.

writing.definition: Words or letters that someone puts on paper.
writing.exampleSentence: I see her writing on the board.

distractors: A picture you draw with colours.
A number you put on a page.
A sound you make with your mouth.

wrong.definition: Something that is not good or not right.

distractors: Something that makes you very happy.
Something that is very big and beautiful.
Something that is good and kind.

yeah.definition: A word you say when you are very happy about something.

distractors: A word you say when you feel very sad.

A word you say when you are very tired.

year.definition: A long time with twelve months in it.

A long time with one hundred days in it.

yellow.definition: A bright colour like the sun or a lemon.

The colour of snow and milk.

yes.definition: A word you say to show you agree or to say something is right.

you.definition: The person or people I am talking to.

A word for a person I do not know.

young.definition: People who are not old, like children and babies.

distractors: People who are very old and have grey hair.

Something that belongs to him or her.

yourself.definition: You and only you, not anyone else.
yourself.exampleSentence: Do it yourself, not with me.
yourself.contextPassage: Yourself means you do something alone. You use yourself when you talk about only you.
distractors: All the people in your family together.
Me and you at the same time.
A person who helps you do things.

ok exclam.,.definition: A word that means yes, good, or I agree. Everything is fine.
ok exclam.,.exampleSentence: OK, I will come to your house today.
ok exclam.,.contextPassage: You say 'OK' when you agree with someone. You also say 'OK' to show that something is good or not a problem.
distractors: A word you say when you are very angry and do not agree.
A word you say when you do not understand what someone said.
A word you say when you want to ask for help from someone.
